title: Sale Psychology
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: How can you resist the psychological pull of sales and discounts?
What strategies help you distinguish between genuine needs and tempting deals?

stages.prompt: A sale tempts you to buy something unplanned. What should you ask?

options.label: Is it cheap?
options.reflection: Just because something is cheap doesn't mean it's a good purchase if you don't need it. A discount doesn't justify unnecessary purchases.

options.label: Will I regret not buying it?
options.reflection: This can lead to impulse purchases. It's better to focus on actual needs rather than fear of missing out.

options.label: Do I actually need it?
options.reflection: Perfect! Asking whether you actually need an item is the most important question before making any purchase, regardless of the discount.

options.label: Is everyone else buying it?
options.reflection: Following trends or others' purchasing decisions often leads to unnecessary expenses that don't align with your actual needs.

stages.prompt: Why do sales make us want to buy more?

options.label: Discounted items are usually higher quality
options.reflection: The quality of an item isn't necessarily improved by being on sale. The discount doesn't guarantee better value.

options.label: Sale items are rare and unique
options.reflection: Sale items are often overstocked or seasonal products, not necessarily rare or unique items. The discount doesn't make them inherently valuable.

options.label: Everything on sale is a great deal
options.reflection: Not everything on sale offers real value. A discounted item is still unnecessary if you don't need it.

options.label: Sales create urgency and fear of missing out
options.reflection: Exactly! Sales tap into psychological triggers like urgency and scarcity, making us feel like we need to act immediately without proper consideration.

stages.prompt: What's the best strategy when encountering an attractive sale?

options.label: Stick to your planned shopping list
options.reflection: Perfect! Sticking to a planned list helps you avoid impulse purchases and ensures you only buy what you actually need.

options.label: Browse and see what catches your eye
options.reflection: Browsing without a plan increases the likelihood of impulse purchases that may not align with your actual needs.

options.label: Buy anything with a high discount percentage
options.reflection: A high discount percentage doesn't make an unnecessary purchase worthwhile. Focus on need, not just the discount.

options.label: Compare prices across multiple stores
options.reflection: While comparing prices is good practice, it doesn't address whether the purchase is necessary in the first place.

stages.prompt: How does the 'sale psychology' affect your finances?

options.label: Sales always help you save money
options.reflection: Sales only help save money if you were planning to buy the item anyway. Otherwise, they often lead to unplanned spending.

options.label: Discounts don't justify unnecessary purchases
options.reflection: Exactly! Discounts can mask the fact that an item is still unnecessary. The core principle is that a good deal on something you don't need is still a bad purchase.

options.label: Sale items are always good investments
options.reflection: Most consumer goods depreciate over time, regardless of discounts. Few items bought on sale maintain or increase their value.

options.label: Sale items should be top priority
options.reflection: Just because something is on sale doesn't mean it should take priority over items you actually need. Needs should always come first.

stages.prompt: What question should you ask before buying anything on sale?

options.label: Do I have enough money for this?
options.reflection: While budget is important, having money available doesn't justify an unnecessary purchase. The focus should be on need, not just affordability.

options.label: Would others think this is a good deal?
options.reflection: Others' opinions shouldn't drive your purchasing decisions. Focus on your actual needs rather than external validation.

options.label: Would I buy this at full price if I really needed it?
options.reflection: Perfect! This question helps you evaluate whether the item has real value to you, regardless of the discount. It focuses on actual need rather than the attraction of a deal.

options.label: Is this a reputable brand?
options.reflection: Brand reputation doesn't change whether an item is necessary for you. Even reputable brands sell items you may not need.
